figures.gloss: The society of alien spirits that seize their hosts — most of them married women — with illness and never leave: Ethiopian nobles, Turkish pashas, European khawajat, Muslim holy men, southern strangers. Each zar has its own drum-rhythm, incense, costume, and appetites, and states its demands through the host's mouth.
figures.facet: A pantheon of foreigners: the zayran are everyone who ever held power over, or passed through, the Nile and Red Sea worlds — possession as an embodied ethnography of the powerful other, which Boddy reads as women's counter-commentary on their own society.
figures.provenance: Janice Boddy, Wombs and Alien Spirits (1989, northern Sudan); Michel Leiris's 1932 Gondar fieldwork (La possession et ses aspects théâtraux chez les Éthiopiens de Gondar, 1958); I. M. Lewis, Ecstatic Religion (1971).

figures.gloss: The cure: days of drumming and incense in which spirit after spirit is called by its own beat until the patient's zar rises and dances in her body, is named, negotiated with — gold, perfume, a new dress, a sacrificial animal — and feasted into peace.
figures.facet: Therapy staged as diplomacy: the ceremony ends not in expulsion but in a treaty — the woman keeps her spirit, the spirit keeps its dues, and the household is put on notice of both.
figures.provenance: Boddy (1989); Gerda Sengers, Women and Demons: Cult Healing in Islamic Egypt (2003); Leiris (1958).

figures.gloss: The woman who runs the cult — shaykha in Sudan, kodia in Egypt, the great mediums of Leiris's Gondar in Ethiopia — herself a former patient whose own zar, once mastered, became her diagnostic partner. She identifies which spirit rides a sufferer and brokers its price.
figures.facet: Authority earned through affliction: the only path to the office runs through having been its patient — a hierarchy of the healed, and one of the few ritual careers open to women anywhere in these societies.
figures.provenance: Boddy (1989); Sengers (2003); Leiris (1958) on Malkam Ayyahou, the Gondar zar mistress.

figures.gloss: The complex's ruling idea: zar spirits are never exorcised, because they cannot be — they can only be identified, honoured, and contracted with. Affliction is converted into relationship, and relationship into a durable claim the possessed woman holds against her spirit and her household alike.
figures.facet: The hinge of a whole scholarly literature: Lewis read it as leverage for the powerless — demands voiced through the spirit that a wife could never voice herself — Boddy as a counter-language of selfhood; either way, the refusal of exorcism is the datum.
figures.provenance: I. M. Lewis, Ecstatic Religion (1971); Boddy (1989); Lewis, al-Safi & Hurreiz (eds.), Women's Medicine: The Zar-Bori Cult in Africa and Beyond (1991).

edges.note: The zar-bori complex (I. M. Lewis): twin African cults of affliction that heal by initiation and accommodation rather than exorcism; diffusion between them is argued — the Sudanese branch is even called 'zar bori' — but unproven, so the seam stays typological.
Women ritual masters of twin possession cults — the Magajiya and the shaykha are both graduated patients who became physicians of the spirit world.
No zar without its beat: the ceremony exists to call each spirit by its own rhythm and bring it, named and negotiable, into the open.

GOLD SEAM: like bori's iskoki, the zayran live inside Islam as a species of jinn — Boddy's Sudanese hosts class them as the 'red wind' — Qur'anic demonology licensing a women's cult that Islamic reformers condemn.

GOLD SEAM: zar's other host faith — in Gondar the cult ran inside Tewahedo Christian households, its mediums baptized Christians (Leiris, 1932); the zayran lodge under the church without ever joining it.

Both cults cure by adoption, not expulsion — the girka tames the iska into a lifelong mount-and-rider marriage exactly as the zar bargain converts affliction into contract; typological twins in Lewis's zar-bori complex, historical diffusion unproven.
Women whose call arrives as sickness and whose cure is the office itself — the shaykha and the Korean mudang both graduate from patient to specialist through the spirit that broke them.

dossier.essence: The zar is a spirit-possession healing complex of the Horn of Africa, the Nile valley, and the Red Sea littoral — Ethiopia, Sudan, Egypt, and the Arabian Gulf — first attested in writing in mid-19th-century Ethiopia and spread along trade and slave routes. Its participants, mostly married Muslim (and in Ethiopia, Christian) women, are seized by zar spirits whose affliction is managed through ceremony but never cured.
dossier.cosmos: The zayran form a parallel society of powerful, mostly foreign spirits — Ethiopian nobles, Turkish pashas, Europeans, holy men — each with its own drum-rhythm, incense, costume, and appetites; within Islam they are accommodated as a species of jinn. They cannot be exorcised: possession is permanent, and the only remedy is identification, negotiation, and contract. The complex carries no eschatology of its own — the host religions keep the afterlife, while zar governs a lifelong this-worldly relationship between woman and spirit.
dossier.practice: The zar ceremony is days of drumming, incense, and dance in which spirit after spirit is summoned by its own beat until the patient's zar rises, names itself through her mouth, and states its price — gold, perfume, clothing, a sacrificial animal — after which it is feasted into peace. Cult life is run by women: the shaykha in Sudan or kodia in Egypt, herself a graduated patient whose mastered spirit became her diagnostic partner, identifies which zar rides a sufferer and brokers its demands; membership, once entered, is for life.
dossier.canon: No scripture — an oral corpus of spirit songs, drum rhythms, and ritual knowledge, documented from mid-19th-century Ethiopian accounts onward; Leiris's 1932 Gondar fieldwork and Boddy's Sudanese ethnography are the classic records.
dossier.reflects: Religion as negotiation from the margins — possession institutionalized as a durable contract through which the socially constrained voice demands and hold standing they are otherwise denied.
sources: Janice Boddy, 'Wombs and Alien Spirits: Women, Men, and the Zar Cult in Northern Sudan' (1989)
I. M. Lewis, 'Ecstatic Religion: An Anthropological Study of Spirit Possession and Shamanism' (1971)
Michel Leiris, 'La possession et ses aspects théâtraux chez les Éthiopiens de Gondar' (1958)
I. M. Lewis, Ahmed al-Safi and Sayyid Hurreiz (eds.), 'Women's Medicine: The Zar-Bori Cult in Africa and Beyond' (1991)

links.cite: Janice Boddy, 'Wombs and Alien Spirits: Women, Men, and the Zar Cult in Northern Sudan' (1989)
